title: Product visual system reset across core surfaces
description: Make NEXUS feel materially different from the earlier baseline by introducing a stronger visual system across the core product surfaces. The goal is not random cosmetic change; it is a clearer, more premium support-triage product identity that makes the value legible without narration. Focus on typography, spacing, color system, shell layout, section hierarchy, and high-signal information cards across queue, inputs, incident, training, and replay.
done_when: the product shell feels visually distinct from the earlier baseline across the core surfaces
the incident screen, inputs screen, and training screen share a coherent visual system instead of looking like separate demos
the new styling improves legibility of the support-triage story instead of only adding decoration

title: Flagship incident console information architecture v2
description: Rework the flagship incident detail experience so an operator can understand the case without narration. Clarify the top summary, evidence flow, mitigation decision, and final approval gate. Make the screen read like a real support investigation console, not a stitched collection of agent outputs.
done_when: INC001 and INC002 both show a clearer top-down operator story from issue framing to Guardian approval
the incident page makes the evidence flow, mitigation ladder, and debugger/trace packet easier to understand without reading long prose blocks
a fresh nxs incident still feels aligned with the same console structure

title: Fresh incident runtime-story parity and stronger live intake framing
description: Deepen the live `nxs_...` intake path so the operator sees stronger runtime-backed comparison language and a clearer support-triage storyline after submitting new evidence. The goal is not to fake parity with seeded incidents, but to keep the fresh path truthful, specific, and visibly aligned with the flagship product story.
done_when: fresh incidents show clearer runtime-backed or scaffold-only language that matches what really executed
the first live incident after /inputs feels closer to the seeded flagship story in structure and usefulness
fresh-path REPLICA, TRACE, FORGE, and GUARDIAN summaries remain semantically aligned after refresh

title: Bounded debugger flow for INC001 beyond the static packet
description: Take the current INC001 debugger packet one step deeper by making it read like an executable debugging flow instead of a static note. Keep it honest and bounded to the curated timeout/retry pack. The output should guide an engineer through the order of checks, expected state transitions, and likely divergence point in a way that materially helps debugging without pretending to be a universal live debugger.
done_when: INC001 exposes a clearer ordered debugging flow with concrete checkpoints and expected state transitions
the UI makes it explicit that this debugger flow is bounded to the curated timeout/retry pack
the docs explain the difference between this bounded debugger flow and a true general-purpose debugger

title: Runtime-host operator UX and pack visibility
description: Productize the runtime-host layer so it is easier for an operator or owner to understand what execution environment exists, which pack was selected, whether runtime replay was delegated, and what the system can and cannot run today. This should reduce hidden mechanics and make the bounded reproduction story more trustworthy.
done_when: the UI surfaces runtime-host and pack status in a clearer product-facing way
operators can tell whether replay used the app host or the runtime host and what environment pack backed it
the runtime-host model feels like an intentional product layer, not a hidden implementation detail

title: Docs and manual demo sync for the runtime-host product layer
description: Once the deeper UI and bounded agent behavior land, update the owner-facing docs so the end-to-end business use case, runtime-host model, debugger boundaries, and manual validation path are easy to follow. The owner should be able to run the Docker flow, open the product, and understand exactly what problem the product solves and what is real versus still bounded.
done_when: the owner-facing docs describe the business use case, runtime-host model, and bounded debugger story clearly
manual steps for Docker, browser, seeded incidents, and fresh incidents are current and complete
the docs make it easy to explain what is real today versus what remains bounded or theoretical
